title: Basic functionality: Visualization, domain detection, and spatial heterogeneity
text: Detecting tissue compartments niches important part study tissue architecture. can applying STclust, spatially-informed clustering method implemented spatialGE. STclust method uses weighted average matrices capture transcriptomic differences among cells/spots. first step STclust, top variable genes identified via Seurat’s FindVariableFeatures, transcriptomic scaled distances calculated using genes. Next, scaled euclidean distances computed spatial coordinates spots/cells. user defines weight (ws) 0 1, apply physical distances. higher weight, less biologically meaningful clustering solution , given clusters reflect physical distances spots/cells less information transcriptomic profiles used. many tests, found weights 0.025 - 0.25 enough capture tissue heterogeneity. default, STclust uses dynamic tree cuts (Langfelder, Zhang, Horvath 2007) define number clusters. users can also test series k values (ks). detailed description method, please refer paper describing spatialGE STclust (Ospina et al. 2022). ’ll try several weights see ’s effect cluster assignments: Results clustering can plotted via STplot function: can see w=0 w=0.05, can detect two tissue niches. w=0.025, gain higher resolution one clusters split third (‘yellow’) cluster appears, potentially indicating different transcriptional profile present . w=0.2, clusters seem compact, indicating weight spatial information probably high. used dynamic tree cuts (dtc) automatically select number clusters, resulting coarse resolution tissue niches, however, users can define range k evaluated, allowing detection tissue compartments.
code: tnbc <- STclust(tnbc,                  ks='dtc',                  ws=c(0, 0.025, 0.05, 0.2), cores=2) cluster_p <- STplot(tnbc,                      samples='sample_094c',                      ws=c(0, 0.025, 0.05, 0.2),                     color_pal='highcontrast') ggpubr::ggarrange(plotlist=cluster_p, nrow=2, ncol=2, legend='right')

what: Association between spatial heterogeneity and sample-level variables
title: Basic functionality: Visualization, domain detection, and spatial heterogeneity
text: explore relationship clinical (sample-level) variable interest level gene expression spatial uniformity within sample, can use SThet() function: SThet function calculates Moran’s statsitic (Geary’s C) measure level spatial heterogeneity expression genes ( NDRG1, IGKC). estimates can compared across samples using function compare_SThet() calculation spatial statistics SThet multi-sample comparison compare_SThet provides easy way identify samples genes exhibiting spatial patterns. previous figure shows expression NDRG1 spatially uniform (lower Moran’s ) across tissues samples patients 2 8 compared patents 9 14. samples higher spatial uniformity expression NDRG1 also tended higher overall survival. Trends less clear IGKC, however, looks like samples expression IGKC spatially aggregated “pockets” (higher Moran’s ) tended lower survival (note patient 9 follow trend). studies using spatial transcriptomics become larger, samples provide insightful patterns association gene expression spatial distribution non-spatial traits associated tissues. computed statistics stored STlist additional analysis/plotting user may want complete. statistics value can accessed data frame using get_gene_meta function: See table simplistic interpretation spatial autocorrelation statistics calculated spatialGE: better understand Moran’s Geary’s C statistics quantify spatial heterogeneity, tissue can simulated using scSpatialSIM spatstat packages. Also tidyverse janitor data manipulation. sc.SpatialSIM package uses spatial point processes simulate locations spots/cells within tissue. facilitate interpretability Moran’s Geary’s C statistics, first tissue simulated, gene expression values simulated. first step create spatstat observation window: Next, sc.SpatialSIM used generate spatial point process (positions spots) within observation window. , assignments spots tissue domains simulated visualized: Now, simulated tissue domain assignments extracted SpatSimObj object. Gene counts simulated way : Expression “gene_1” exclusive “Tissue_1”. spot assigned “Tissue 1”, expression “gene_1” 1 (high expression). assigned “Tissue 2”, expression “gene_1” 0.1 (low expression). Given “Tissue 1” spots aggregated towards center tissue, expected Moran’s > 0 Geary’s C < 1 “gene_1”. Spots high expression “gene_2” (expression = 1) equally separated spots low expression (expression = 0.1). pattern highly unlikely biological tissue. expected pattern yields Moran’s < 0 Geary’s > 1. Expression “gene_3” results randomly assigning expression “gene_2” across entire tissue. pattern result Moran’s closer 0 Geary’s C closer 1. visualize simulated expression run SThet, STlist created: expected, simulated expression “gene_1” aggregated (.e., clustered, red spots concentrated towards center tissue). result intently obtained setting simulation parameters xmin=2, xmax=3, ymin=2, ymax=3 sc.SpatialSIM. results metrics expected: “gene_1” shows aggregation/clustering, indicative “hot-spot” expression. “gene_2” “gene_3” show uniform random expression respectively. Notice none values far “random” expectation (Moran’s = 0 Geary’s C = 1). One reasons result effect library-size normalization. addition, obtaining extreme values C require extreme spatial patterns, unlikely observed even way data simulated .
code: tnbc <- SThet(tnbc,                genes=c('NDRG1', 'IGKC'),               method='moran', cores=2) p <- compare_SThet(tnbc,                     samplemeta='overall_survival_days',                     color_by='patient_id',                    gene=c('NDRG1', 'IGKC'),                     color_pal='muted',                    ptsize=3)  p get_gene_meta(tnbc, sthet_only=TRUE) library('rpart') library('spatstat') library('scSpatialSIM') library('tidyverse') library('janitor') wdw <- owin(xrange=c(0, 3), yrange=c(0, 3)) sim_visium <- CreateSimulationObject(sims=1, cell_types=1, window=wdw) # Generate point process # Then, simulate tissue compartments set.seed(12345) sim_visium <- GenerateSpatialPattern(sim_visium, gridded=TRUE, overwrite=TRUE) %>%    GenerateTissue(k=1, xmin=1, xmax=2, ymin=1, ymax=2, sdmin=1, sdmax=2, overwrite=TRUE)  PlotSimulation(sim_visium, which=1, what="tissue points") +    scale_shape_manual(values=c(19, 1)) # Extract tissue assignments from the `SpatSimObj` object # Simulate expression of 'gene_1' sim_visium_df <- sim_visium@`Spatial Files`[[1]] %>%   clean_names() %>%    mutate(gene_1=case_when(tissue_assignment == 'Tissue 1' ~ 1, TRUE ~ 0.1))  # Generate expression patter of "gene_2" for(i in 1:nrow(sim_visium_df)){   if(i%%2 == 0){     sim_visium_df[i, 'gene_2'] = 1   } else{     sim_visium_df[i, 'gene_2'] = 0.1   } }  # Generate expression of "gene_3" # Set seed for resproducibility set.seed(12345) sim_visium_df[['gene_3']] <- sample(sim_visium_df[['gene_2']]) # Extract simulated expression data sim_expr <- sim_visium_df %>%    add_column(libname=paste0('spot', seq(1:nrow(.)))) %>%   select(c('libname', 'gene_1', 'gene_2', 'gene_3')) %>%   column_to_rownames('libname') %>% t() %>%    as.data.frame() %>% rownames_to_column('genename')  # Extract simulated spot locations sim_xy <- sim_visium_df %>%    add_column(libname=paste0('spot', seq(1:nrow(.)))) %>%   select(c('libname', 'y', 'x'))  # The `STlist` function can take a list of data frames simulated <- STlist(rnacounts=list(sim_visium=sim_expr),                      spotcoords=list(sim_visium=sim_xy), cores=2)  # Plot expression ps <- STplot(simulated, genes=c('gene_1', 'gene_2', 'gene_3'), data_type='raw', color_pal='sunset', ptsize=1) ggpubr::ggarrange(plotlist=ps, ncol=3) # The `SThet` function requires normalized data simulated <- transform_data(simulated, cores=2)  # Run `SThet` simulated <- SThet(simulated, genes=c('gene_1', 'gene_2', 'gene_3'), method=c('moran', 'geary'))  # Extract results get_gene_meta(simulated, sthet_only=TRUE)

title: Seurat Integration: Bidirectional Workflows
text: vignette demonstrates integrate spatialGE Seurat seamless spatial transcriptomics workflows. spatialGE package provides bidirectional conversion utilities allow : Convert Seurat objects STlist - Run spatialGE analyses Seurat-processed data Convert STlist Seurat - Leverage Seurat’s visualization downstream analysis tools Run spatialGE directly Seurat - One-step analysis workflows Transfer results back Seurat - Add cluster assignments, spatial statistics Seurat metadata

title: Seurat Integration: Bidirectional Workflows
text: Make sure packages installed: Load required libraries:
code: # Install spatialGE devtools::install_github("ACSoupir-OC/spatialGE")  # Install Seurat (if not already installed) install.packages("Seurat") library(spatialGE) library(Seurat) library(Matrix) library(tibble)

title: Seurat Integration: Bidirectional Workflows
text: vignette, ’ll create synthetic STlist object demonstrate workflow:
code: set.seed(42)  # Create synthetic count matrix (20 genes x 100 cells) n_genes <- 20 n_cells <- 100  counts_mat <- Matrix::Matrix(   matrix(rnbinom(n_genes * n_cells, mu = 5, size = 2),           nrow = n_genes, ncol = n_cells),    sparse = TRUE ) rownames(counts_mat) <- paste0("Gene", 1:n_genes) colnames(counts_mat) <- paste0("Cell", 1:n_cells)  # Add some marker gene names rownames(counts_mat)[1:5] <- c("MLANA", "CD37", "TP53", "GAPDH", "ACTB")  # Create spatial coordinates coords_df <- data.frame(   barcode = paste0("Cell", 1:n_cells),   xpos = runif(n_cells, 0, 1000),   ypos = runif(n_cells, 0, 1000) )  # Create STlist object st_obj <- methods::new("STlist",   counts = list(test_sample = counts_mat),   spatial_meta = list(test_sample = coords_df),   sample_meta = tibble::tibble(sample_name = "test_sample"),   gene_meta = list() )  cat(sprintf("Created STlist: %d genes x %d cells\n", n_genes, n_cells)) #> Created STlist: 20 genes x 100 cells

# Convert STlist to Seurat object seurat_obj <- as.Seurat.STlist(st_obj, verbose = FALSE)  # Inspect the Seurat object cat(sprintf("Seurat object:

The names of the list are the names of each gene set # The contents of each list element are the gene names within each gene set gene_sets <- split(x=gene_sets[['gene_symbol']], f=gene_sets[['gs_name']]) gene_sets = gene_sets[grep('SIGNALING', names(gene_sets))] stenrich_df <- STenrich(lung, gene_sets=gene_sets,                          reps=1000, or equal than 0.3 of genes from a gene set present in sample  res <- bind_rows(stenrich_df) %>%   mutate(prop_gene_set=size_test/size_gene_set) %>%   filter(prop_gene_set >= 0.3 & adj_p_value < 0.05) %>%   mutate(slide=str_extract(sample_name, "Lung5_Rep1|Lung6")) %>%   select(slide, gene_set) %>%   mutate(gene_set=str_replace(gene_set, 'HALLMARK_', ''))  # Generate barplot showing the number of FOVs with siginificant evidence of spatial # patterns for each tissue slide ggplot(res) +    geom_bar(aes(x=gene_set)) +   xlab(NULL) +   theme(axis.text.x=element_text(angle=70, vjust=1, hjust=1)) +   facet_wrap(~slide) bind_rows(stenrich_df) %>%   mutate(prop_gene_set=size_test/size_gene_set) %>%   filter(prop_gene_set >= 0.3 & adj_p_value >= 0.05) %>%   arrange(desc(prop_gene_set), desc(adj_p_value)) %>%   filter(gene_set == 'HALLMARK_NOTCH_SIGNALING') %>%   filter(str_detect(sample_name, "Lung5_Rep1")) %>%   slice_head(n=3) bind_rows(stenrich_df) %>% Next, removes isolated spots/cells reference domain (.e., number immediate neighbors smaller parameter min_nb). min_nb parameter intends reduce effects small “pockets” reference domain correlation coefficient. point, either minimum average distance non-reference cell reference cell calculated (distsumm). minimum distance easier interpret, however, sensitive highly fragmented reference domains. hand, interpretability average distance easy minimum distance, might detect better way gradients reference domain distributed across sample. Finally, Spearman’s coefficient calculated addition regression line coefficient. slope indicates direction correlation. positive, gene expression tends higher farther reference domain. negative, gene expression higher closer reference domain. interpretation sign Spearman’s coefficient similar, however magnitude indicates strong correlation gene expression distance reference domain. Now, find genes significant spatial gradients across three samples, commands can used: can seen results multiple keratin genes show spatial gradients, negative Spearman’s coefficients (cells close domain 3 tend higher keratin expression). HLA-B NDRG1 higher expression cells far domain 3: results genes within NOTCH signaling pathway: Although correlations genes pathway large KRT6A HLA-B (.e., lower absolute Spearman’s coefficient), can seen genes NOTCH1 NOTCH3 show evidence expression gradients FOVs ‘Lung6_fov_4’ ‘Lung6_fov_6’.
code: colnames(lung@spatial_meta$Lung5_Rep1_fov_2) stg <- STgradient(lung,                    topgenes=1000,                    annot='stclust_spw0.02_dsplFalse',                   ref=3,                   samples=c('Lung6_fov_4', 'Lung6_fov_6', 'Lung6_fov_7'),                    distsumm='min',                    robust=TRUE, cores=2) head(stg$Lung6_fov_4) bind_rows(stg) %>%   arrange(desc(abs(min_spearman_r))) %>%   group_by(sample_name) %>%   slice_head(n=5) %>%   ungroup() %>%   arrange(gene) qp2 <- STplot(lung, genes=c('KRT6A', 'HLA-B'), samples='Lung6_fov_4', color_pal='YlOrBr') ggpubr::ggarrange(plotlist=qp2, common.legend=TRUE) bind_rows(stg) %>%   arrange(desc(abs(min_spearman_r))) %>%   filter(gene %in% gene_sets[['HALLMARK_NOTCH_SIGNALING']]) %>%   filter(min_spearman_r_pval_adj < 0.05)

title: spatialGE: Spatial Genomics Analysis
text: R package visualization analysis spatially-resolved transcriptomics data, generated 10X Visium. spatialGE package features data object (STlist: Spatial Transctiptomics List) store data results multiple tissue sections, well associated analytical methods : Visualization: STplot, gene_interpolation, STplot_interpolation explore gene expression spatial context. Spatial autocorrelation: SThet, compare_SThet assess level spatial uniformity gene expression calculating Moran’s /Geary’s C qualitatively explore correlations sample-level metadata (.e., tissue type, therapy, disease status). Tissue domain/niche detection: STclust perform spatially-informed hierarchical clustering prediction tissue domains samples. Gene set spatial enrichment: STenrich detect gene sets indications spatial patterns (.e., non-spatially uniform gene set expression). Gene expression spatial gradients: STgradient detect genes evidence variation expression respect tissue domain. Spatially-informed differential expression: STdiff test differentially expressed genes using mixed models spatial covariance structures account spatial dependency among spots/cells. also supports non-spatial tests (Wilcoxon’s T-test). methods initial spatialGE release, technical details, utility presented publication: https://doi.org/10.1093/bioinformatics/btac145. details recently developed methods STenrich, STgradient, STdiff please refer spatialGE documentation.

Default=0.2 clusters cluster name(s) test DE genes, opposed clusters. pairwise whether carry tests pairwise manner. default pairwise=F, meaning DE genes tested comparing cluster rest pooled cell/spots. verbose either logical integer (0, 1, 2) increase verbosity cores Number cores use parallelization. title: STgradient: Tests of gene expression spatial gradients — STgradient
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # ============================================================================ # STEP 1: Load TNBC test data and create STlist # ============================================================================ # Load TNBC test data from package data_path <- system.file("tests/testthat/data/tnbc_bassiouni", package = "spatialGE")  # Get file paths for all 8 samples samples_dirs <- list.dirs(data_path, recursive = FALSE) count_files <- character(length(samples_dirs)) coord_files <- character(length(samples_dirs))  for (i in seq_along(samples_dirs)) {   count_files[i] <- file.path(samples_dirs[i],                                "GSM6433585_092A_filtered_feature_bc_matrix.h5")   coord_files[i] <- file.path(samples_dirs[i], "spatial",                                "GSM6433585_092A_tissue_positions_list.csv") }  # Load clinical data clin_file <- file.path(data_path, "bassiouni_clinical.csv")  # Create STlist object st_obj <- STlist(rnacounts = count_files,                  spotcoords = coord_files,                  samples = clin_file)  # Transform data (variance stabilizing transformation) st_obj <- transform_data(st_obj) # Expected output: STlist object with 8 samples, transformed counts  # ============================================================================ # STEP 2: Run spatial clustering to get tissue domain annotations # ============================================================================ # Run STclust to identify tissue domains st_obj <- STclust(st_obj, ws = 0.025, ks = c(2, 3), cores = 1, verbose = FALSE) # Expected output: Adds 'stclust_spw0.025_k2' and 'stclust_spw0.025_k3' columns  # ============================================================================ # STEP 3: Define direction vector for spatial gradient analysis # ============================================================================ # Extract spatial coordinates coords <- st_obj@spotcoords[[1]]  # Calculate gradient direction (conceptual vector from one edge to another) dir_x <- diff(range(coords$x))  # Total x range ~5000 microns dir_y <- diff(range(coords$y))  # Total y range ~5000 microns  # Direction vector represents gradient from tissue edge to edge # Example: gradient from lower-left (0,0) to upper-right (max_x, max_y) # This defines the spatial axis along which we test gene expression gradients  # ============================================================================ # STEP 4: Run STgradient to detect gradient genes # ============================================================================ # Analyze sample 1, using cluster 1 as reference domain result <- STgradient(   x = st_obj,   samples = 1,   topgenes = 100,  # Use fewer genes for quick demonstration   annot = "stclust_spw0.025_k2",  # Column name from STclust output   ref = "1",  # Reference cluster (tissue domain)   distsumm = "min",  # Use minimum distance metric   cores = 1,   verbose = TRUE ) # Expected output: List with 2 data frames (k=2 and k=3 clustering results)  # ============================================================================ # STEP 5: Identify and visualize top gradient genes # ============================================================================ # View first 10 genes (sorted by Spearman correlation rho) print(head(result[[1]], 10)) # Expected output: Data frame with columns: gene, rho, p.value, adjusted.p.value  # Identify top positive gradient genes (expressed higher closer to reference) top_positive <- result[[1]][order(-result[[1]]$rho), ][1:5, ] print(top_positive) # Expected output: Top 5 genes with strongest positive correlation to proximity  # Identify top negative gradient genes (expressed higher farther from reference) top_negative <- result[[1]][order(result[[1]]$rho), ][1:5, ] print(top_negative) # Expected output: Top 5 genes with strongest negative correlation to proximity  # ============================================================================ # STEP 6: Visualize gradient gene expression patterns # ============================================================================ # Plot expression of top positive gradient gene top_gene <- top_positive$gene[1] gene_expr <- st_obj@counts[[1]][top_gene, ]  # Create spatial plot data plot_data <- data.frame(   x = coords$x,   y = coords$y,   expression = gene_expr,   cluster = st_obj@spatial_meta[[1]]$stclust_spw0.025_k2 )  # Create spatial scatter plot with ggplot2 library(ggplot2) ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = x, y = y, color = expression)) +   geom_point(size = 0.5) +   scale_color_viridis_c() +   labs(title = paste("Expression of", top_gene),        color = "Expression") +   theme_minimal() # Expected output: Spatial heatmap showing gene expression gradient  # ============================================================================ # STEP 7: Compare different distance metrics # ============================================================================ # Run with average distance metric result_avg <- STgradient(   x = st_obj,   samples = 1,   topgenes = 100,   annot = "stclust_spw0.025_k2",   ref = "1",   distsumm = "avg",   cores = 1,   verbose = FALSE )  # Compare correlations between min and avg distance metrics cor(result[[1]]$rho, result_avg[[1]]$rho) # Expected output: Correlation coefficient (~0.95-1.0 for highly correlated genes) } # }

code: # Example using TNBC test data included with spatialGE if (requireNamespace("spatialGE", quietly = TRUE)) {   data_dir <- system.file("tests/testthat/data/tnbc_bassiouni", package="spatialGE")   if (data_dir != "") {     # Get file paths for first sample     sample_dir <- list.dirs(data_dir, recursive = FALSE)[1]     count_files <- list.files(sample_dir, pattern="\\.h5$", full.names = TRUE)     coord_dir <- file.path(sample_dir, "spatial")     coord_files <- list.files(coord_dir, pattern="tissue_positions", full.names = TRUE)     clin_file <- list.files(data_dir, pattern="clinical", full.names = TRUE)          # Create STlist object     tnbc <- STlist(rnacounts = count_files,                     spotcoords = coord_files,                     samples = clin_file,                    verbose = TRUE)          # Inspect object     tnbc     summary(tnbc)   } }

Users can input regular expression query gene names calculate percentages (example % mtDNA genes). function also can filter entire samples. Note function removes cells/spots, genes, /samples raw counts, transformed counts, spatial variables, gene variables, sample metadata. Also note function filters following order: Samples (rm_tissue) Spots (rm_spots) Genes (rm_genes) Genes matching rm_genes_expr Min max counts

title: spatialGE 2.0.0 (2026-03-22)
text: STlist: Data loading Visium, CosMx, CSV/TSV STplot: Gene expression cluster visualization SThet: Moran’s spatial heterogeneity analysis STclust: Tissue domain detection DynamicTreeCut STdiff: Differential expression (Wilcoxon, t-test, spatial mixed models) STenrich: Gene set enrichment permutation testing STgradient: Spatial gradient detection Spearman correlation STclust modular architecture 5 helper functions STdiff modular architecture (non-spatial + spatial tests) Replaced base R hclust() fastcluster::hclust() 2-5x speedup Expected 20-35% speedup STclust runtime STclust_select_genes(), STclust_calculate_distances(), STclust_weight_distances() STclust_hierarchical(), STclust_get_hier_clusters_*() STdiff_run_nonspatial(), STdiff_fit_spatial_models(), STdiff_compile_results() Core functions exported flexibility C++ code Seurat’s FindVariableFeatures implementation C++ code STenrich computations (GSVA integration)

title: spatialGE 2.0.0 (2026-03-22)
text: STenrich can now calculate gene set enrichment scores via GSVA STenrich can test gene set “hot-spots” within specific tissue domains STgradient supports log-transformed spatial distances New functions: spatial_metadata(), tissue_names() quick metadata access Example datasets now spatialGE_Data repository Use DelayedArray memory-efficient calculations

title: spatialGE 2.0.0 (2026-03-22)

title: spatialGE 1.2.1
text: CRAN release: 2025-05-26 Added C++ code speed STenrich computations (Thank , Dr. Soupir!). STenrich function can now calculate gene set enrichment scores (via GSVA) addition gene set average expression. STenrich function can now test gene set “hot-spots” within specific tissue domain (annot domain arguments). Spatial distances can now log-transformed STgradient (log argument). Example data sets now spatialGE_Data GitHub repository. New functions spatial_metadata tissue_names quickly access names spot/cell annotations sample names. Re-assessed package dependencies. Use DelayedArray calculations. Several bug fixes.
